# Blog Writing Style Guide

applyTo: "_posts/**"

## Voice & Tone

- **Direct and confident.** No hedging, no filler. Say what you mean.
- **Pragmatic realism.** Acknowledge both sides (risks and opportunities), but take a clear position. Never overly optimistic, never doom-and-gloom.
- **Professional but human.** Write like a senior engineer talking to peers at a conference — not like a textbook, not like a LinkedIn influencer.
- **No corporate speak.** Avoid buzzwords without substance. If you use a term like "agentic workflow," explain what it means concretely.
- **Slightly opinionated.** The author has a point of view and is not afraid to state it. But opinions are always grounded in reasoning, never arbitrary.

## Perspective & Pronouns

- Default to **second person** ("you", "your") when addressing the reader.
- Use **third person** for general observations ("Engineers will need to…", "Juniors are in a difficult position.").
- Use **"I"** sparingly — only when sharing a genuinely personal experience or project. The latest analytical posts avoid "I" almost entirely.
- Never use "we" to vaguely include the reader. Be specific about who you mean.

## Sentence Style

- **Short to medium sentences.** Rarely more than 20 words.
- **Declarative statements** dominate. State facts and positions plainly.
- **Fragment sentences for emphasis** are allowed and encouraged. ("In reality, it is both.", "It is decline.")
- **No contractions.** Write "do not" instead of "don't", "cannot" instead of "can't", "it is" instead of "it's". This gives the text a deliberate, considered quality.
- **Active voice** unless passive is clearly better. "AI handles that" not "that is handled by AI."

## Paragraph Structure

- **Short paragraphs.** 1–3 sentences per paragraph. Never a wall of text.
- **Single-sentence paragraphs** are a deliberate stylistic tool, especially for emphasis or transitions. Use them.
- **No transition phrases.** Do not write "Furthermore," "Moreover," "In addition," "It is worth noting," "Let us dive in." Just state the next point.

## Opening

- **Jump straight into the topic.** The first sentence makes a bold, clear claim or observation. No "In this article, I will discuss…" framing.
- The opening sets the stakes immediately. The reader knows within two sentences what this piece is about and why it matters.
- Example pattern: bold statement → brief context → "For many X, this feels like Y. For others, Z. In reality, it is both."

## Headings & Structure

- **H2 (`##`) for main sections.** Keep the heading hierarchy flat. Use H3 only when a section genuinely has sub-parts.
- Headings are **short and descriptive** — not clickbait, not questions (with rare exceptions). Examples: "The End of Coding as the Core Identity", "What Skills Become More Important", "What Happens Next: New Roles Will Emerge".
- Every post follows a **clear arc**: context → analysis → implications → actionable takeaway.

## Lists

- Use **bulleted lists** for unordered collections of concepts.
- Use **numbered lists** when order or priority matters.
- List items start with a **bold keyword or phrase**, followed by a brief explanation on the same line or the next line.
- Keep list items roughly equal in length. Each item is 1–2 lines, not a full paragraph.
- Do not overuse lists. Prose is the default; lists are for when items genuinely stand in parallel.

## Bold & Emphasis

- **Bold** is used for key terms and concepts when they are introduced or are central to the argument. ("agent orchestrators", "junior agentic operators")
- Do **not** bold entire sentences or use bold for general emphasis.
- *Italics* are rarely used. Reserve for subtle stress or for titles of external works.
- No ALL CAPS for emphasis. Ever.

## Closing / Conclusion

- The closing is **punchy and actionable.** Short imperative sentences directed at the reader.
- Pattern: a series of direct commands or observations, each on its own line. Example:
  > Observe the evolution continuously.  
  > Adapt your skill set faster than before.  
  > Stay open to AI even when sentiment is skeptical.
- End with a **strong final statement** — a single sentence that sticks. ("Standing still is not neutral. It is decline.")
- Do not write "In conclusion," or "To summarize," — just deliver the closing.

## Formatting Rules

- **No emojis** in analytical or opinion posts. (Emojis are acceptable only in casual announcement posts like a welcome post.)
- **Code blocks** with language identifiers only when discussing a technical implementation. Do not include code for decoration.
- **Links** are used sparingly and only when they add genuine value (e.g., linking to a project repository).
- **Horizontal rules** (`---`) only at the very end before a closing note, if needed.
- **No "About the Author" block** inline. The author's identity comes from the front matter.

## Language

- Write in **English**. Clear, accessible English — not overly academic, not dumbed-down.
- Vocabulary is precise. Use the right technical term, but explain it if it is niche.
- Avoid filler words: "very", "really", "actually", "basically", "just", "quite", "literally."
- Avoid "AI speak" giveaways: "delve", "landscape", "leverage", "harness", "paradigm shift", "game-changer", "dive into", "at the end of the day", "it goes without saying."

## What NOT to Do

- Do not start with "In today's rapidly evolving world…" or any variation of it.
- Do not use rhetorical questions as section openers unless you answer them immediately and concretely.
- Do not pad sections to seem longer. If a point is made in two sentences, move on.
- Do not insert motivational quotes from famous people.
- Do not write a summary paragraph that restates every section heading.
- Do not use "Let us" or "Let's explore" as transitions.
- Do not produce disclaimers like "This is just my opinion" — the reader knows it is your blog.
